The Hidden Data Leaks Threatening Universities

This investigation arose from a post found in a Breach Forums monitoring, where a URL of a Mexican university was mentioned, allowing access to the data despite having a login.

All the results found were based on the information found in the aforementioned post, which served as a basis for creating Google dorks that allowed for searches such as:

  1. intitle:”index of” fotos alumnos : Find exposed directories with photographs of students
    2. inurl: edu.mx intitle:”index of” *.zip : Find zipfiles on sites with the domain edu.mx
    3. inurl:edu.mx intitle:”index of” .zip & .php : Allows you to find backups in zipformat of phpcode.
    4. inurl:.edu.mx intitle:”index of” *.sql : Allows you to find SQLfiles from the education system, often database dumps.

As can be observed, these are quite simple dorks, but due to the exposure of these sites, they allow finding a large amount of sensitive information. Additionally, the data resulting from the dorks can be used as a pivot to move to other directories and obtain other data or paths to continue the collection. For example, several of the explored paths are not the base directory, which allows moving up the directory hierarchy to the base or entering subdirectories.

Another aspect used in these tests to reach more sections of the educational systems was removing the subdomain in the URLs that contained it, which redirected to the main site. For example, some of those found had this format https://mail.universidad.edu.mx, removing the subdomain mail and leaving only the URL as https://universidad.edu.mx. In some cases, it redirected to the main site, which allowed discovering more routes or vulnerable code.

The tests conducted are limited to discovering exposed data and analyzing the information derived from these findings, as well as the frontend code found on the pages, if permitted. No reconnaissance was performed on the infrastructure of these domains or sites found, such as port scanning or mapping of domain name records.

As a measure to protect the privacy of the institutions and individuals whose data were found, the complete URLs are not shown, and the photographs of individuals and personal data have also been censored.

Broken Access Control

In all the analyzed cases, the found sites were exposed directories, which demonstrates a misconfiguration, as sometimes the site may be protected by a login portal that grants access to a system from which the data is generated or obtained. However, if one directly accesses the directory path with the data, it bypasses this authentication, allowing access to the data from the unprotected routes.

For example, the site mentioned in the Breach Forums post was obtained with the dork intitle:"index of" fotos alumnos, the result of the dork gave a URL whose directory title has a format like index of /fotos/alumnos, this matches the dork, but if you visit the main path below where this directory is, it redirects to a login portal, but any path and subdirectory of the exposed part is accessible without authentication.

Public directories leaks

Some of the exposed directories contained files that are created to be publicly downloadable, they are assets of the pages, or they are paths to PHP files necessary for the functioning of web applications. In any of these cases, having a directory with public access is not a security flaw since such public access is necessary. However, the flaw found is that these directories contained files that should not be public, for example, code backups in ZIP formats, or SQL dumps of the databases.

These leaks can occur due to ignorance, bad practices, or irresponsibility on the part of sysadmins and developers of educational systems, but also because of the type of data found and the structure of the paths of these directories. The leaks may be there to facilitate access to the data, which leaves the information unprotected.

La Información filtrada

Personal Data

In one of the analyzed sites, the direct result of the dork led to a path with photographs of the students. Given the type of images, they appear to be identification photos, and the name of each file is the CURP of the person appearing in the image. When going to the parent directory, a subdirectory called /personal was found, which contains photographs of the workers in a format similar to that of the students, also named with a CURP.

Searching on Google with a simple dork like this "<CURP>" using any in the student directory yields a URL with a list of all the students in the school, in this list you can see the data:
- Full name
- CURP
- Specialty
- Degree
- Group

One aspect that makes this exposure delicate is that the institution is a high school, so many of its students are minors and with the mentioned list, the full name is already available, which if used in a search can yield more personal information, such as documents or public registry data.

For example, in the case of workers, no list was found as such, but in Mexico, with just the CURP, one can obtain the person’s full name from public records, as is the specific case of https://www.gob.mx/curp/. This provides more information that can be used to personalize phishing attacks.

As a demonstration, one of the workers’ photographs was used as a starting point to obtain their CURP. From this, it was searched on the previously mentioned public records site. With the full name obtained thru Google searches, the professional license number of the person in question was obtained, as well as the name of the university they graduated from and their profession.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The search for the name also led to a document from the educational institution, which exposes the data, revealing another publicly accessible route with more downloadable documents. In this specific case, the found document mentions the person, along with the position they hold in the educational institution and their professional title, something that can be corroborated with the data from the public records of the professional license.

In another case, a leak of some python scripts used to electronically sign school documents was found, but to perform these actions a key is required. In this case, the element used as a key is a FIEL or Advanced Electronic Signature. This file is exposed in a directory called FIEL and the file name is a RFC (Federal Taxpayer Registry). Upon examining its content, there is a private key. This information can be abused by attackers to commit fraud since the FIEL can have the validity of a physical signature and is widely used in official procedures.

Source Code

A backup can preserve the state of a system, protecting it from poorly applied changes or data loss, but storing them in a location with no access controls, leaving these files exposed to the public, allows attackers to obtain a version of the school control system, which is often the most up-to-date, enabling a complete analysis of the code, finding attack vectors and highly vulnerable components. Additionally, this simplifies the entire reconnaissance process as a reverse engineering process of the APIs is not necessary since all the code is already available.

Among our findings, most of these systems are developed in PHP, which, when we find the code to be executable, does not allow us to read it. However, we found cases where there are ZIP files with backups of the source code with a difference of less than a year between the backup and the files in use. This indicates that they are not so outdated and allows for a study of the internal functioning of the web application.

Something that was identified in one of these cases is poor handling of the parameters passed to SQL queries, which allows for SQL injection attacks, vulnerabilities for which there is already quite a bit of useful information to mitigate and many solutions within the technologies used that are not new but help in secure software development by avoiding these problems.

The site with the SQLi vulnerability, within the code, has a condition where a username is used that we will not show for privacy reasons, but which can be used to exploit the vulnerability with a simple payload as USER' OR 1=1#.

Original Code

As can be seen in the image, there is no validation or sanitization of the data sent by the user, and it is passed directly to the query parameters. Therefore, the previously mentioned payload, containing ', truncates the text string, making it possible to replace the rest of the statement with a condition that evaluates to true, and the comment at the end # leaves the rest of the query unexecutable. The following image shows how the injection appears in the code.

Code with injection

This returns the corresponding user data and allows them to log into the system. There are several ways to mitigate this type of vulnerability, from the simplest, such as cleaning text strings or using preparation methods to serialize the parameters used in SQL requests and prevent code injection.

When working with SQL databases, dumps are often created, which are complete copies of the information and contain not only the data but also the entire database structure, allowing it to be replicated on another server. These types of files were also found, allowing an attacker to access the information backed up in the database.

It is very important to protect access to these files, as these institutions handle information belonging to other people, such as students and employees. By being so irresponsible with the handling of this information, they allow anyone to obtain it, putting the individuals involved at risk, for example in dumps such as these, where there is personal information such as:
- Name
- Address
- Contact details
- CURP

Since it is not only the structure of the tables that is exposed, but also the data in INSERT statements, this allows for a serious leak of this information.

Credentials

Within the sites reviewed, we found database connection data, mainly MYSQL, used in PHP and python, all with the data hardcoded in the code or generating exportable libraries, as in the case of those in PHP, or in .env, which are exposed with all the environment variables.

Some tests were carried out to determine the severity and find out if an external party could make the connection, but the servers had blocked these services externally, which, although a good security practice, does not make it 100% secure, since if an attacker gains access to one of these servers, they could access the database with these credentials. Furthermore, although the passwords appear to be secure due to the way they are formed, their storage method is not secure, as they are visible to anyone who finds them.

Another example of credential exposure is this one, where there is a JSON file apparently used to configure the backend, which establishes the API routes of the web application and some authentication credentials to be able to use the services.

As a method of validating the exposed credentials, we tested sending a form simulating an authentication request using cURL with the following payload.

The response retrieved contains authentication tokens that are widely used as a control method in web applications. In this case, it is a bearer token that is normally sent in an authorization header in this way: authorization: bearer <token> .

Therefore, when testing by sending the previously obtained token to one of the JSON routes, the request responds with the data of the user to whom the API credentials correspond, and according to one of the returned parameters, it indicates that this user is active.

One of the protected routes in the JSON that was censored in the image refers to a URL that provides student details using the CURP as an identifier and parameter in the same URL.

Using system functions with exposed data

There are systems with sections or features that can only be used if the correct parameters are provided, such as a specific identifier. Finding the necessary parameters may be possible by analyzing the filtered source code, as in the cases mentioned earlier in this report, but this identification can also be done by reviewing the data already available in the exposed directories.

Some Mexican universities use security measures for physical access to their facilities. These can be implemented using physical credentials linked to student accounts, as will be seen below, or with another personal identifier such as the CURP.

One of the subdirectories found contains QR codes that are most likely used in student identification, as there are templates for these credentials in other paths that contain the student’s photograph and a QR code very similar to those found. When reviewing the content stored in the QR code, a link with this format is obtained: https://<url_base>/<school>/constancia.php?curp=<curp>&fecha=<date> .

If you click on this link, you will obtain a PDF file. This is an academic certification that includes the full name of the student corresponding to the CURP used as a parameter in the URL, as well as the degree program, grade, and group in which the student is enrolled. If the CURP parameter is changed to another one, such as those found in the student photo directory, another PDF file is generated with the corresponding student’s information. This is an example of the files that can be accessed.

It is important to mention that in the same directory with the above-mentioned QR codes, there are records named with payloads of SQLi injections, which is an indicator of a file upload or generation endpoint, in this case QR codes, that was abused by a potential attacker. and due to the way the flow works when generating PDFs by calling any of these files, if the system is vulnerable to SQL injections, it can be exploited when searching for the CURP.

Reviewing some of these payloads, it can be seen that one of the reasons why there are many of these records is a possible test to determine the type of database used, since some of them contain statements such as PG_SLEEP used in Postgres, DBMS_PIPE used in Oracle databases, and when performing SQLi attacks, it is extremely important to identify the version and type of database running on the server to determine the payloads to use.

In other systems, it is possible to cause errors that provide a lot of information with very detailed logs, which can be used by attackers to find attack vectors. As mentioned above, the use of exposed code makes it possible to identify vulnerabilities, but even if these exposures did not exist, it is still possible to find them with such descriptive information in the error messages.

This is one of the cases where an error is referenced in which the connection to the database cannot be made, possibly because the connection is disabled or the credentials have been changed, but an important piece of information that can be used to exploit vulnerabilities is the full paths that appear in the error messages.

Applicable measures

Due to the origin of the data shown here, many of the leaks can be resolved with appropriate access control, adding permissions to web directories, and treating all data as private by default, only giving public access to data and files that require it.

The handling of personal information, especially when it belongs to third parties, requires great care and responsibility, as it can put at risk the people whose data is exposed, especially if, as mentioned in this report, those involved are in many cases minors. A lack of vision in how an attacker can use data that apparently has no use results in little or no protection of this information. Exposing photographs with identifiers such as CURP, RFC, or full names can be used, for example, in identity theft, especially if supplemented with other information found in different exposures or public records.

Code backups are another element that must be well protected due to the extensive information they provide to attackers in terms of system recognition. Leaving them in compressed files such as ZIP is bad practice, as in terms of development it is not an optimal way to manage application versions. You can implement the use of third-party repositories such as github or use solutions that can be implemented on your own servers, such as GitLab or Bitbucket.

The use of these repository services, together with git as a version control system, can prevent the accidental leakage of sensitive data such as credentials by configuring them correctly, for example with gitignore, or by reviewing the changes that are uploaded to the repositories themselves.

Regular monitoring of systems can reveal malicious or unwanted activity, giving sysadmins an idea of the vulnerabilities they are exposed to. For example, in the case of the Breach Forums post, which is the basis for this investigation. At the time of conducting all these tests and reviews, there were still interactions from forum users, so it is very likely that the system is being exploited by attackers, something that can only be identified through security reviews or until an attack is so noticeable that it stops the operation of the system or leaks data to a cybercrime site.
